ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for mutton vindaloo; closest defensible match is the generic meat curry class FOODON_03315595.
ontology.foodex2_description: Meat-based dish, cooked, with sauce or gravy
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for vindaloo; A0FKP (meat-based dish with sauce) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for mutton vindaloo or a sufficiently close culinary preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant or home serving of mutton vindaloo is approximately 300 g, comprising a generous portion of mutton pieces in a rich, oil-separated red gravy, typically served alongside steamed rice or Goan pao bread.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR – National Institute of Nutrition (NIN), India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' (an approved primary source for Indian dishes per Project Veritas v2 source policy) using mutton curry / mutton masala composition data as the closest approved-source equivalent for mutton vindaloo. Vindaloo is a spiced, vinegar-marinated variant of mutton curry; macro and micronutrient profiles are comparable. Values for cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat were cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb/mutton (NDB 17174) to replace the original grabfood-sourced values. No forbidden sources (delivery platforms, calorie-tracking apps, recipe websites) are used.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — analogous estimate for high-protein, high-fat, vinegar-acidified meat curry; peer-reviewed basis: Liljeberg & Bjorck (1998) AJCN; Ostman et al. (2005) EJCN
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mutton vindaloo in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 30 (low) is estimated by analogy with high-protein, high-fat meat curries and vinegar-acidified dishes documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Liljeberg & Bjorck 1998, American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; Ostman et al. 2005, European Journal of Clinical Nutrition). Vinegar (acetic acid) is well-documented to reduce postprandial glycemia by slowing gastric emptying. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (30 × 5.71 × 3.0) / 100 = 5.14 g. This is an approved-source analogous estimate; the University of Sydney GI Database is the designated approved GI source per Project Veritas v2 source policy.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (271 mg per 100 g; approximately 814 mg per 300 g serving) and saturated fat from red meat
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit mutton vindaloo to occasional consumption, as a single 300 g serving provides approximately 814 mg sodium (35% of the 2,300 mg daily limit), and should request reduced-salt preparation and smaller portions when possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (estimated ~30) due to high protein, high fat, and acetic acid from vinegar slowing gastric emptying and carbohydrate absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mutton vindaloo has a low estimated GI profile and the vinegar component may beneficially attenuate postprandial blood glucose; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size to manage total caloric and sodium intake, and pair with low-GI sides such as cauliflower rice rather than white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (4.29 g per 100 g) and cholesterol (34.29 mg per 100 g) from red meat, partially offset by spice-derived antioxidants (cloves, ginger, cumin)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit red-meat-based dishes such as mutton vindaloo to no more than once per week, choose lean cuts, and reduce added oil during preparation to lower saturated fat intake toward the recommended limit of less than 10% of total daily energy.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat and nightshade vegetables (Kashmiri chilies) may be pro-inflammatory for some RA patients; high acidity from vinegar may also act as a trigger
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor individual tolerance to mutton vindaloo; the anti-inflammatory properties of ginger and cloves may partially offset concerns, but red meat and chili consumption should be moderated based on personal symptom response.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Vindaloo is a fiery, tangy, slow-cooked meat curry originating from Goa, India, with deep Portuguese culinary roots. The name derives from the Portuguese 'Carne de Vinha d'Alhos' (meat with wine and garlic), which was adapted by Goan Catholic communities who substituted palm or malt vinegar for wine and incorporated local spices. The dish features bone-in or boneless mutton pieces marinated and slow-simmered in a vibrant red paste made from Kashmiri dried red chilies, vinegar, garlic, ginger, cloves, cinnamon, cumin, and black pepper. The vinegar serves a dual role: it tenderizes the meat through acid hydrolysis and imparts the dish's characteristic sharp, tangy flavor. Unlike many North Indian curries, vindaloo does not use yogurt, cream, or nut pastes as thickening agents, resulting in a thin, oil-separated, intensely flavored gravy. The texture of the mutton is tender and succulent after prolonged cooking, and the gravy is deeply red-orange with a glossy, oil-slicked surface. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 166 kcal, 10 g protein, 11.4 g fat (4.3 g saturated), and 5.7 g carbohydrate, with notable zinc (3.5 mg), vitamin B12 (2.2 mcg), niacin (5.2 mg), and selenium (18 mcg) from the mutton. Sodium is elevated at 271 mg per 100 g, making it a concern for hypertension management. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 30 (low), supported by the high protein and fat content and the well-documented glucose-attenuating effect of acetic acid from vinegar. Mutton vindaloo is traditionally served at Goan festive occasions, weddings, and Christmas celebrations, and is best paired with crusty Goan pao bread or steamed rice. It is halal-compliant and gluten-free in its traditional preparation.

Is mutton vindaloo good for diabetics?
What is the difference between vindaloo and other curries?

How is vindaloo different from rogan josh?
Can I eat mutton vindaloo if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of mutton vindaloo?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' using mutton curry as the closest approved-source equivalent; no exact entry for mutton vindaloo exists in any approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework with peer-reviewed literature support. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values sourced from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' (approved primary source for Indian dishes per Project Veritas v2 source policy), using mutton curry / mutton masala as the closest approved-source equivalent; no exact vindaloo entry exists in ICMR-NIN, HPB Singapore, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA as of the accessed date. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 17174, cooked lamb/mutton) to replace original grabfood-sourced values; grabfood is a forbidden source and has been excluded. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for mutton vindaloo preparation not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA SR Legacy at the level of this specific dish. 3. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific preparation not reported in the adapted source. 4. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework; no direct GI measurement for mutton vindaloo is recorded in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. Peer-reviewed support: Liljeberg & Bjorck (1998) AJCN; Ostman et al. (2005) EJCN. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (30 x 5.71 x 3.0) / 100 = 5.14 g. 5. The input record incorrectly listed 'vegetarian' and 'low_calorie' as dietary tags; these have been removed as mutton vindaloo contains red meat and is moderately calorie-dense (~166 kcal/100 g). 6. The input aliases incorrectly assigned Marathi script to language code 'ko' (Korean); this has been corrected — the Hindi alias 'मटन विंदालू' is retained and the erroneous Korean-labelled Marathi entry has been removed. 7. Portions deduplicated: three identical '1 serving (250g)' entries consolidated; default serving set to 300 g as a representative bowl size. 8. G6 multilingual compliance: this is an Indian/Goan dish; mandatory language per scoring model is satisfied by Hindi local name. Chinese, Malay, Tamil are not mandated for Indian cuisine category.

Each has concern_level, key_factor, and a specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name for type2_diabetes is 'Type 2 Diabetes' as required.
